question_text: A fixed container of helium gas at 20°C has a pressure of 100 kPa. The temperature is increased to 40°C. Calculate the new pressure, assuming the volume and amount of gas remain constant. (Hint: use absolute temperature in Kelvin)
correct_answer: 106.7 kPa (or approximately 107 kPa). Convert to Kelvin: 20°C = 293 K, 40°C = 313 K. Using P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂: 100/293 = P₂/313, so P₂ = (100 × 313)/293 = 106.8 kPa
explanation: This is a multi-step problem requiring conversion to absolute temperature (Kelvin), then applying Gay-Lussac's Law. When temperature increases, particles move faster and hit the container walls harder and more often, increasing pressure proportionally.
